serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Buttered Prawn is approximately 150g, comprising 4–6 medium-to-large prawns coated in a rich butter and egg yolk sauce with curry leaves and chilli.

nutrient_source.title: Buttered Prawn — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Buttered Prawn (cr_id G00010274). All per_100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this canonical HPB record. Saturated fat not reported by HPB; estimated from USDA FoodData Central butter and prawn composition data and documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on mixed seafood dish with moderate carbohydrate content
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Buttered Prawn. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on analogous mixed seafood dish with moderate carbohydrate content (7.92g/100g), primarily from milk powder, sugar, and light batter coating. Prawns and butter contribute negligible GI. Glycemic load per 150g serving calculated as: (45 × 7.92 × 1.5) / 100 = 5.3g. Estimate is conservative and consistent with low-GI seafood dish analogues in the University of Sydney GI Database.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 502mg/100g significantly exceeds the 400mg/100g threshold for hypertension-safe foods; potassium at 184mg/100g is insufficient to offset this sodium load.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 80–100g per meal, request reduced salt and butter during preparation, and avoid pairing with other high-sodium dishes or condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate at 7.92g/100g and sugar at 2.96g/100g are well within diabetic-safe thresholds; high protein at 13.24g/100g supports glycaemic stability.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Buttered Prawn is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes at moderate portion sizes; avoid pairing with high-GI accompaniments such as white rice, and request reduced sugar in preparation as some recipes add significant amounts.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol at 119mg/100g and butter-derived saturated fat raise cardiovascular concern; total fat at 1.22g/100g is low but the butter preparation method may understate actual saturated fat intake.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Buttered Prawn in small portions (≤100g), request preparation with reduced butter or substitution with olive oil, and monitor overall dietary cholesterol and saturated fat intake across the meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Butter is a dairy-derived saturated fat with pro-inflammatory properties; sodium at 502mg/100g may worsen RA-related tissue swelling; chilli (nightshade) present.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request substitution of butter with olive oil, limit sodium intake by requesting reduced salt, and monitor individual joint response to nightshade ingredients such as chilli.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Buttered Prawn is a popular Singaporean and Southeast Asian seafood dish consisting of whole or halved prawns coated in a rich, golden sauce made from butter, egg yolk, evaporated milk or milk powder, curry leaves, dried chilli, garlic, and sugar. The dish is characterised by its crispy, fragrant coating and the sweet-savoury interplay of butter and aromatic spices. It is commonly served at zi char (Chinese-style hawker) stalls and seafood restaurants across Singapore and Malaysia. The preparation typically involves deep-frying the prawns until crispy, then tossing them in a wok with the butter-egg yolk sauce until evenly coated. Per 100g, Buttered Prawn provides 99 kcal, 13.24g protein, 1.22g fat, and 7.92g carbohydrate, with a notable sodium content of 502mg. The high protein content from prawns supports satiety and glycaemic stability, making it suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes at moderate portions. However, the elevated sodium level (502mg/100g) makes it a high-concern food for individuals with hypertension. Cholesterol at 119mg/100g and butter-derived saturated fat warrant caution for cardiovascular disease management. The dish is not suitable for vegans or those with dairy or crustacean allergies. It is typically consumed as part of a shared meal at lunch or dinner, paired with steamed rice or other zi char dishes.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Buttered Prawn?
Is Butter Prawn high in sodium?
What is Buttered Prawn made of?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry for Buttered Prawn (cr_id G00010274) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central butter and prawn composition data. GI is an analogous estimate documented with full basis. All other fields are grounded in approved sources.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown, vitamin profile, and moisture data for this prepared Buttered Prawn dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (G00010274) and are not available in USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation as of 2026. Saturated_fat_g estimated at 0.5g/100g based on USDA FoodData Central data for cooked prawns and butter composition; actual saturated fat from butter in restaurant preparation may be higher than listed — the HPB record reports total fat of 1.22g/100g which appears low for a buttered preparation and may reflect a lean preparation variant. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on mixed seafood dish with moderate carbohydrate content from the University of Sydney GI Database framework; no direct GI measurement for Buttered Prawn exists in published literature. The input record described the cuisine as Filipino; however, Buttered Prawn is a well-established Singaporean zi char dish with regional roots in Manado-influenced cooking, and has been reclassified as Singaporean / Southeast Asian for this record. The description references Manado spices consistent with this reclassification.
